segment-text: Written and Directed by
As-Produced Script
EXT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - VIRGINIA - NIGHT - COLD OPEN - FLASH
FORWARD A windowless CIA off-site facility. Armed security guard the entrance.
How long have you been working on this project, Dan?
DANIEL JONES (PRE-LAP)
CLIFFORD (PRE-LAP) INT. OFF-SITE BASEMENT - WINDOWLESS ROOM - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
DANIEL JONES (30s) enters a secured room. CLIFFORD (PRE-LAP)
Dan flips on the lights, revealing the walls -- covered with documents and black and white mugshot-like photographs. DANIEL JONES (PRE-LAP)
Dan approaches a desk. He opens a drawer and takes out an envelope with the words "Panetta Review" written on it.
INT. CLIFFORD'S CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - FLASH FORWARD INTERCUT
Dan with attorney CY CLIFFORD (40s).
Do you have a relationship, Dan? Family? Just trying to get a better sense of who you are.
When it started, I was seeing someone. But, you know, the nature of the work and the hours...
INT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - STAIRWELL - NIGHT - INTERCUT
Dan jogs up the stairs with haste, holding a locked document bag.
I wasn't a very good partner.
Five years -- and it could all go away...
INT. CLIFFORD'S CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - INTERCUT

Just vanish in the face of these allegations. I could certainly imagine an ambitious young man like yourself spends years with his head buried in some terrible spectacle, gets frustrated with the process,
sees an opportunity to get the
Or, I could see an equally ambitious senator encouraging you to do something. Now would be a good time to tell me.
That did not happen. What I did, I did on my own.
INT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - SECURITY AREA - NIGHT - INTERCUT
I acted alone.
Dan walks through the lobby and scans his ID card. We will see this scene again. OFF-SITE SECURITY
I used to. Got in the way of work.
Dan moves toward the door, but the security guard stops him.
Have a good night, Jay. OFF-SITE SECURITY You too, Dan.
Dan walks out.
EXT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - VIRGINIA - CONTINUOUS
Dan, with his document bag over his shoulder, hurries toward the parking lot.
So you did steal the document?
I did not steal it; I relocated it.
Everyone has their own words...
INT. CLIFFORD'S CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - INTERCUT
The language is built to choose sides. Now, why did you "relocate" it? Beat.
They can send you to jail, Dan.
INT. DANIEL JONES' CAR - NIGHT - INTERCUT
Dan gets in and throws the document bag onto the passenger seat. He takes a deep breath. Pauses. SUPER: THE TORTURE REPORT The word "TORTURE" is then redacted.
I think I should start at the beginning.
EXT. CAPITOL BUILDING STEPS - MORNING
Daniel Jones -- clean-cut and earnest, in his mid-20s -walks up the steps and pauses to take a photo of the Capitol. He keeps going.
INT. HART SENATE BUILDING - HALLWAY - MOMENTS LATER
Dan sits on a bench, looking at something in his hands... ANGLE ON A SNOW GLOBE OF THE US CAPITOL The snow swirls around inside the souvenir in Dan's hands. DENIS MCDONOUGH, 40s, approaches. Harried.
You Dan Jones? Yeah.
Denis McDonough.
Denis extends a hand.
Hi, thank you so much for meeting me.
Come on in. Yeah, absolutely.
Dan follows Denis into the office.
INT. DENIS MCDONOUGH'S SENATE OFFICE - MOMENTS LATER
Dan sits with Denis McDonough, who has Dan's resume in front of him. An informal interview.
My second day of grad school was September 11th. I was headed to a lecture and everything just...stopped. The next day, I changed all my classes to National Security.
Good man. So, what's the long-term plan? Where do you see yourself in 10 years? You run for office?
Oh, no, no politics for me. I think I'd be more effective behind the scenes. Somewhere I can really make a difference.
So what about before Harvard? What'd you fill up your days with then?
Teach for America for three years in Baltimore. Seventh graders. I read that you taught high school Spanish in St. Paul, Minnesota after college.
And LBJ's first job was teaching at the Mexican school in south Texas. Knowing how to deal with children is a highly useful skill when working on the hill.
Dan laughs.

But, from my perspective as Senate staff, I would wait until things turn around up here. I think Senator Daschle would say the same thing. Go get some real world experience in counterterrorism, in foreign policy -- try the CIA, try FBI.
Have them teach you what they don't teach in a classroom. Then, you can come back here and help us fix all the things that Bush and Cheney are
breaking-The phone rings and Denis answers.
He covers the phone.
(sotto to Dan) The boss. Here. Keep in touch, huh?
He gives Dan a business card.
Thank you very much.
I do not have that information right now, but I can certainly get that for you in the next fifteen.
Dan leaves forgetting the snow globe on Denis' chair.
EXT. HART SENATE BUILDING - MORNING
Dan, a bit older now, approaches the entrance. DANIEL JONES (PRE-LAP)
INT. SENATE CONFERENCE ROOM - LATER
Dan briefs a few other SENATE STAFFERS who take notes.
...with 1,500 pounds of hydrogen peroxide and 26 military-grade detonators. The CIA suspects the Islamic Jihad Union--
MARCY, Chief of Staff to Senator Dianne Feinstein, pokes her head into the meeting.
Excuse me. Can I borrow you for a minute, Dan? The Senator would like to see you.
Yeah, of course.
Dan gathers his things.
INT. SENATOR FEINSTEIN'S BULLPEN - MOMENTS LATER
Marcy and Dan walk through. SENATOR FEINSTEIN (PRE-LAP) Morning, Dan. DANIEL JONES (PRE-LAP)
INT. SENATOR FEINSTEIN'S OFFICE - CONTINUOUS
Dan, Marcy, and SENATOR DIANNE FEINSTEIN (70s) -- very serious and formal.
Have you seen the story today in the New York Times?
No, Senator. I have not.
Marcy hands Dan the paper.
Evidently, the CIA destroyed tapes of interrogations of al-Qaeda detainees.
Did the Intel Committee know there were tapes?
No. This is the first I've heard of it. So, I want to find out what was on the tapes -- why they were destroyed.
We want you to lead the investigation, Dan.
The Senator nods.

But, if the tapes were destroyed, how do we-SENATOR FEINSTEIN They're saying they have written records -- thousands of pages. Let's see about that. I want you to find out exactly what they have and read every word of it.
Dan nods.
INT. HART SENATE BUILDING - HALLWAY - MORNING
Dan signs in at a desk in front of the Senate SCIF. He carries a locked document bag. PRE-LAP: sound of a gavel. SENATOR FEINSTEIN (PRE-LAP)
Dan enters the SCIF. AN IMPOSING METAL DOOR closes behind him.
INT. SENATE SCIF - MOMENTS LATER
Dan sits at the witness table, facing the 15 SENATORS OF THE INTELLIGENCE COMMITTEE -- each seated behind a placard with their name.
Based on these revelations, a larger study into the entire CIA Detention and Interrogation program is clearly in order. All in favor say yay. Mr. Whitehouse? Yay.
Yay.
SSCI CLERK (O.S.) SENATOR BURR (O.S.)
Mr. Rockefeller? Yay.
Mr. Chambliss? Nay. Mr. Risch?
The yays accumulate off screen.
Now, I think this is a waste of time...
INT. SENATOR FEINSTEIN'S OFFICE - LATER
Chambliss and Feinstein discuss the new investigation.
I know you voted against this,
You already had your tapes
Yes, but the rest of us voted to go further, 14-1. It is our job to provide oversight.
All during the campaign, you got candidate Obama saying how he
wanted to be the first postpartisan President.
Saxby, you know me. It's my intention that this report be seen as coming from the entire Intelligence Committee. Not just from the Democrats.
So, who takes the lead?
Well, I was thinking Dan Jones-20
INT. DC ST. REGIS - RESTAURANT - MORNING
Marcy slides a DCI ID card with Dan's photo across the table to Dan.
He spent four years doing counterterrorism at the FBI. He's already got the security clearances.
Marcy gives Dan instructions on his new assignment.

The rules are the same as the tapes investigation. No politics. No bias. And the Senator isn't interested in opinions or theories -just facts. Based on what you found out already, this could go to a pretty dark place. You need to keep your personal feelings out of it -- that's the first thing Republicans are gonna look for. No talking to the press, or your girlfriend, or any family members. Nobody outside of the Committee.
Dan nods.
You're going to get a team of six -three Republicans, three Democrats... (MORE)
but there can't be any Republican sentences or Democratic
And how long do we have?
We're hoping you can wrap it up in a year or so...
EXT. VIRGINIA ROAD - NEXT DAY
Dan drives his car to his new workplace.
You're going to have to keep the Senators updated on your work. Nobody wants any surprises.
EXT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - VIRGINIA - LATER
Dan walks toward an entrance where SEAN MURPHY (40s) awaits him.
There's a covert CIA off-site in Virginia. You start tomorrow.
Jones, right?
Sean? Hi, I'm Dan.
They shake hands.
Sean Murphy from the Agency. Let's go inside.
They walk toward the entrance.
INT. OFF-SITE LOCATION - SECURITY AREA - MOMENTS LATER
Dan and Sean enter the facility.
OFF-SITE SECURITY
Sean swipes his ID, as does Dan.
The room we've designated is a SCIFno phone reception. No photos. You
know the drill. Lead walls. THEY DESCEND THE STAIRS
And per the agreement, the room is off limits to everyone except Committee personnel.
Absolutely. No one inside without your permission.
THEY WALK DOWN A HALLWAY
And the computers?
Right here. Your own dedicated server. Air-gapped work stations.
They arrive at a SERVER built into the wall.
We'll be updating the database as we go -- the files will be loaded on here as we collect them from across the Agency.
Great. We'll want all the relevant documents as soon as possible.
Well, we gotta vet it first. There's a lot to-DANIEL JONES Vet? Director Panetta agreed to give us everything pertaining to the program. Who -- who would be vetting it?
It's a big Agency -- we gotta make sure you don't get anything you're not supposed to.

They arrive at a door with a combination pad on it and a sign: UNITED STATES SENATE INTELLIGENCE COMMITTEE STAFF ONLY. BY ORDER OF THE DIRECTOR OF CENTRAL INTELLIGENCE. Sean punches in a code and opens the door.
INT. OFF-SITE BASEMENT - WINDOWLESS ROOM - CONTINUOUS
Dan surveys the space. A very grey, sterile room with SIX DESKS and SIX COMPUTER MONITORS. Spotless. Dan clicks a lamp on and off, then plays around with a keyboard.
Make yourself at home.
There's no printer. No paper.
No documents are allowed to leave the room without CIA approval. Paper has a way of getting people in trouble at our place.
I think at some point we're gonna need a printer. And paper. At our place, paper is how we keep track of laws. Beat.
DANIEL JONES (CONT'D)
Dan walks out, Sean follows.
INT. OFF-SITE BASEMENT - WINDOWLESS ROOM - LATER

Dan sits at his computer. Fellow STAFFERS JULIAN, APRIL, and THREE REPUBLICAN STAFFERS all sit at desks surrounding Dan. Dan types "September 11" and "al-Mihdhar" into the search engine. Hits enter. The results pop up: CIA internal documents, memos, emails. Dan reads off his computer screen. Flashes of documents, words and images pertaining to 9/11 dart across the screen, taking us to...
INT. COUNTERTERRORISM CENTER - FLASHBACK SUPER: 11 SEPTEMBER 2001, COUNTERTERRORISM CENTER

A room full of distraught CIA OFFICERS stare at a bank of MONITORS playing 9/11 footage in a sickening loop. AUDIO of NEWS COVERAGE of the terrorist attacks is heard in the background. Phones continue to ring, but no one answers them. GRETCHEN (30s) sits at a desk. She is approached by an ANALYST who hands her a folder.
You're not gonna like this.
And with that, Gretchen stands and approaches BERNADETTE, 40s, intense and grim.
We confirmed some of the names.
The hijackers?
Gretchen hands her the folder.
Two of the guys from the Malaysia meetings -- the San Diego guys. They took down flight 77. Pentagon. Beat. This settles in on Bernadette hard.
She looks up at the monitors -- the towers begin to collapse as they turn to ash. THOMAS EASTMAN (40s), CTC CHIEF COUNSEL, enters.
Attention everyone. We have reason to believe that the Counterterrorism Center is a potential target. We are asking all personnel to evacuate. Immediately.
Everyone begins to leave, but Bernadette remains in place, in a state of shock and disbelief. Filled with regret. Finally, she places the folder on a desk and walks out.
INT. DIRECTOR TENET'S OFFICE - ANOTHER DAY - FLASHBACK SUPER: CIA HEADQUARTERS, SEPTEMBER 16, 2001
Push past a sign on the door: DIRECTOR CENTRAL INTELLIGENCE: GEORGE TENET. COFER BLACK, Counterterrorism Director, wanders in. Inside, Tenet watches Meet the Press.

We also have to work sort of the dark side, if you will. We're going to spend time in the shadows in the intelligence world. A lot of what needs to be done here will have to be done quietly, without any discussions... CHENEY (TV) (CONT'D) Using sources and methods that are available to our intelligence agencies if we're going to be successful...
Fucking warned them -- August 6th Presidential briefing: "bin Laden Determined to Strike in U.S." Flashing red lights!
And before that, in July, what did we say? "There will be significant terrorist attacks against the United States in the weeks and months to come." It's not your fault.

It is a mean, nasty, dangerous, dirty business out there, and we have to operate in that arena. I'm convinced we can do it. We can do it successfully. But we need to make certain that we have not tied the hands, if you will, of our intelligence communities.
INT. CIA CONFERENCE ROOM - TWO DAYS LATER - FLASHBACK
Tenet, Cofer, CIA CHIEF COUNSEL JOHN RIZZO, Thomas Eastman, and others gather.

Counterterrorism Director Black and I saw President Bush at Camp David this weekend -- and the President gave me his word that there will be no changes in CIA leadership as a result of Tuesday's attacks. We will look forward now -- and not waste time second guessing, or looking for blame. Going forward, we will be the tip of the spear in the battle against al-Qaeda.
As I said to the President -- two weeks, we'll have flies walking
across al-Qaeda eyeballs.
I've asked Chief Counsel Rizzo here to walk us through where we are from a legal standpoint.
The President has issued a Memorandum of Notification giving CIA the power to capture and detain suspected terrorists on the battlefield.
Detain them where? Are we bringing them to the US?
We're really not in the prison business over in Counterterrorism.
Look, the budget for Counterterrorism last week was, what, 600 million dollars? The

segment-speaker: budget this week is whatever you
segment-text: need it to be, and we are in whatever business it takes to get the job done.
Initially CIA legal stated...
INT. SENATOR FEINSTEIN'S CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - PRESENT
Dan briefs Senator Feinstein and Marcy, the first of many such meetings.
...that any facility used to hold detainees had to meet the requirements of the US Bureau of Prisons. But, then Deputy Director Pavitt thought that was going to be too limiting.
So, who came up with black sites?
Not totally clear. My guess is either a senior officer at Counterterrorism -- CTC -- or ALEC station bin Laden unit decided black sites were the best option.
Well, did the CIA tell the State Department they were making deals to hold people on foreign soil? Where was Secretary of State Powell in all this?
Well, I found a memo -- the CIA's General Counsel said the decision was made not to tell Powell about the program because...
Dan leafs through some documents. Finds a quote.
He would "blow his stack if he were to be briefed on what was going on."
Well, who decided that?
Someone inside the White House.
Mhm. What about interrogations?
President Bush's memoranda just authorized capturing and detaining - it didn't say anything about interrogations.
And who gave them permission to play that role?
Nobody. Looks like it started with legal at CTC. Beat.
Do you want me to brief Senator McCain on this? I know he's interested-SENATOR FEINSTEIN The Republicans just pulled out of the study, Dan.
INT. OFF-SITE BASEMENT - WINDOWLESS ROOM - NEXT DAY
April walks in. Notices only half the staff are now here.
Good morning.
Where is everybody?

Attorney General Holder opened a criminal investigation into the CIA. As a result, the Agency won't allow any of their people to be interviewed for our report. The Republicans don't see how we can move forward, so they're out.
So does this mean we stop? I mean,
the Committee voted to investigate-DANIEL JONES The Senator says we keep going.
But, we can't actually speak to anyone who was involved in the program.
Right. According to CIA legal, CIA personnel is only speaking to DOJ.
And is DOJ going to share their findings? I mean, can we work with them?
I've reached out to the special prosecutor -- I'm waiting to hear back.
Okay. But, if nobody who actually worked on the program is going to talk to us, how do we investigate?
So, we have their files, their emails and cables, their memos. We have to use their own communications to tell the story.
Do you have any idea how hard that's gonna be? The tapes investigation took two years, Dan, and this is about a lot more than what was on the tape.
Yeah, we don't even know what 'this' is.
Right, but we do know who it starts with. We go one detainee at a time.
Dan returns to his computer. April and Julian follow suit.
Dan types "Abu Zubaydah" "March 2002" "Detention Site Green"
into the search engine. Flashes of documents, words and images pertaining to Abu Zubaydah dart across the screen, taking us to...
EXT. AIRSTRIP - DETENTION SITE GREEN - NIGHT - FLASHBACK SUPER: DETENTION SITE GREEN, MARCH 2002

ABU ZUBAYDAH, strapped to a gurney, is wheeled from a plane on a palm tree lined airstrip. He is met by a small group of MEN. One of them is ALI SOUFAN of the FBI. Ali says something to Zubaydah in Arabic. No answer.
You speak English? Zubaydah moans from a fresh gunshot wound.
My name is Ali Soufan. I'm with the FBI...You're under arrest. Do you understand?
Where are you taking me?
Zubaydah is transported into a large van.
INT. DETENTION SITE GREEN - INFIRMARY - DAY - FLASHBACK
Ali Soufan sits with his PARTNER and Abu Zubaydah, who is still in CRITICAL condition from his wounds. Ali gently puts blocks of ice on Zubaydah's lips.
Your wound is infected, but we are going to take good care of you. Beat.
I want to show you something...
He shows Zubaydah a film clip on a toughbook. It's of bin Laden speaking to members of al-Qaeda.
He's talking to someone off screen. He's calling him "Mukhtar." You know who that is?
Zubaydah looks at Ali, then at Ali's partner. He's trapped,
(Arabic) Mukhtar. The man whose brain flew away. Like an airplane.
The man who lost his mind.
Ali's partner hands Ali a book of photos. Ali shows Zubaydah.
No, not him.
Zubaydah leafs forward a few pages. Points to a photo. Ali conceals his surprise.
Khalid Sheikh Mohammed? Ali nods at his partner -- they got it.
(Arabic) We'll get the doctor in to see you. Then I'll come back and we'll talk more. Pepsi?
Zubaydah nods.
(Arabic) Am I going to live?
(Arabic) God willing.
Ali and his partner walk out.
INT. DIRECTOR TENET'S OFFICE - LATER - FLASHBACK
Tenet, Cofer, and Bernadette.
Abu Zubaydah confirmed that KSM is the leader of all al-Qaeda operations outside of Afghanistan. He trained the hijackers -- we're calling him the mastermind.
Outstanding! Hats off to the bin Laden group. (MORE)
Whichever one of your people did this -- I want to call and congratulate them in person -- who was it?
Beat. Bernadette isn't sure what to say. Looks to Cofer.
Wasn't our guy.
What? Who got it?
FBI is working Abu Zubaydah.
An FBI guy got this?
Yes. Ali Soufan -- they used him in the Cole investigation. He's part of their counterterrorism group -he speaks Arabic. Tenet explodes.

I don't care who speaks Arabic! This is not just a criminal investigation. We are gathering intelligence here -- that is what we do. The FBI is looking at the past; we need to stop attacks in the future. I want CIA in the room with this guy, okay? I want CIA in
the room with Zubaydah! INT. CTC - CONFERENCE ROOM - LATER - FLASHBACK
Eastman, Bernadette, Gretchen and Cofer.
So, who do we have that can work on him? CIA runs sources -- people who cooperate. That's not these guys.
Every second we are not draining the detainee's brain, we are rolling the dice.
We need our people in the room. We need to be the ones asking the questions now.
I heard from someone over in Technical Services, a woman named Miriam... about a couple of Air Force psychologists who came in and pitched a whole new approach to interrogations: Jim Mitchell and
Bruce Jessen. Miriam says Mitchell
INT. CTC - CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - FLASHBACK
JAMES MITCHELL (40s) and BRUCE JESSEN (50s) present their interrogations plan to Eastman, Bernadette and NEW CTC DIRECTOR JOSE RODRIGUEZ.
Zubaydah didn't tell us everything because we stopped short of where we needed to go.
Mitchell clicks through a powerpoint presentation.

SERE stands for survival, evasion, resistance and escape. It's a program to help prepare U.S. Special Forces for whatever they might encounter if captured. Now, I have over fourteen thousand hours observing SERE interrogation
techniques. Bruce here has more
Observing? As opposed to-JESSEN I took a course in conducting interrogations as well. I've worked with volunteers in actual simulations.
We feel we can reverse-engineer these SERE techniques to induce learned helplessness.
Can you explain what that means: "reverse-engineer"?
Instead of using these techniques to protect our people, we use them to achieve compliance in the detainees.
Our plan involves using individual assessments of detainees to create a program designed to induce debility, dependency, and dread.
We call these the three D's.
Mitchell nods.
And there's science to back this up?
Oh, yes, yes sir.
Excuse me, Dr. Mitchell. Can you elaborate on the techniques you'll be employing?
Absolutely.
Mitchell moves ahead in his powerpoint. Each technique has a corresponding slide.
The attention grasp, walling,
facial hold.
The facial slap or insult slap, cramped confinement, wall standing, stress positions.
Sleep deprivation, waterboarding.
Use of insects and mock burials. Beat. The room shifts uncomfortably.
Well, from my perspective, this seems like it could be a potential game changer. But, of course, it's
I think if we're serious about stopping the next threat, we need to put on our big boy pants and provide the authorities these guys need.
These approaches might raise some
Well, we're not at Defense, we're at the CIA. Besides, you just said, we use this SERE stuff on our own guys all the time. I don't see a problem.
Mitchell and Jessen nod.
INT. OFF-SITE BASEMENT - WINDOWLESS ROOM - PRESENT
Dan reads off of his computer screen. Julian and April stand behind him.
Shortly, after the CIA's decision to engage Dr. Mitchell, Abu Zubaydah was moved to a cell that was lit 24 hours a day. Loud noise was fed into his cell to disrupt his sleep...
What about the guy who got him talking? Ali... Soufan?
Wait. He's FBI, not CIA. So we can ask him questions, right? No rule against that.
We can try.
EXT. ONE WORLD TRADE - NEW YORK CITY - MORNING The new World Trade Center under construction on a cloudy
day.
EXT. CENTRAL PARK - BETHESDA FOUNTAIN - MORNING
Ali sits on the edge of the fountain. Dan walks toward him and sits down.
There's only one interrogation technique that works: rapport building. You get close to these guys, and they open up. But, the CIA didn't believe that. Just look at Abu Zubaydah.
Well, you got him to talk. How'd you do that?

I walked in one day and played him a tape of himself. Most of it was of a phone conversation -- we had tapped his phones but he forgot to hang up at the end of the call -so we had all this background noise of his house. Then, my partner walked in with a big box full of cassette tapes. We told him we had bugged his safe house. We had been listening for months. We knew everything, so lying was pointless.
He looked at the box full of tapes
And all the tapes were blank?
Of course they were! But, he didn't know that. So, he talked.
What happened when the contractors showed up?
Ali's demeanor changes. Unsettled.
INT. D.S.G. - CHIEF OF STATION OFFICE - DAY - FLASHBACK
Ali with Bernadette, Mitchell, and GARY THE CIA INTERROGATOR. SUPER: APRIL 2002
Who the fuck is this?
Dr. Mitchell is an Air Force psychologist and he's developed a new method of interrogation.
Why -- why do we need a new method?
He's not telling you everything.
We think he knows more -- we put him at number 3 or number 4 in alQaeda leadership -- he probably
knows the next move.
Do you speak Arabic, Jim? Does Gary here?
Don't need to.
Can I ask you something? Have you ever interrogated any sort of extremist before? Know much about al-Qaeda? Nope.
Have you ever interrogated any sort of terrorist before? No.
Any sort of criminal?
Have you ever interrogated anyone before?
It's not important. He's a human. I'm a psychologist. He knows a secret, and I'm going to get him to tell that secret to Gary.
What are you gonna to do to him that isn't-MITCHELL What am I gonna do? What is Jim gonna do? Jim's gonna do what
INT. DETENTION SITE GREEN - HOLDING CELL - ANOTHER DAY FLASHBACK
LOUD HEAVY METAL plays on speakers. A naked and bruised Zubaydah is chained in a standing stress position. Guards dressed in hoods and all black forcefully unchain Zubaydah and drag him out.
Let's go shitbird!
Move it, asshole.
INT. DETENTION SITE GREEN - HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS - FLASHBACK
The hooded guards drag Zubaydah down the hall. He pleads for his life in Arabic. He is thrown into the interrogation cell.
INT. D.S. GREEN - INTERROGATION CELL - CONTINUOUS - FLASHBACK 42
Zubaydah is tossed onto a metal chair. The guards grab his hair to hold him in place as they begin to shave him. ANGLE ON a tripod camera in the cell recording the scene.
INT. D.S. GREEN - OBSERVATION BAY - CONTINUOUS - FLASHBACK
Mitchell, Ali, Bernadette, and physician's assistant RAYMOND NATHAN (40s) watch a monitor as the guards shave Zubaydah's beard and head.
You can't just shave a Muslim man. Do you know what that means? He's going to shut down.
Gotta humiliate him right off the bat. Same reason we took away his clothes.
Why the loud music?
Sleep deprivation. We stop the
music when he starts talking. Then he can sleep.
Tired people tell the truth?
Learned Helplessness. You're familiar with Dr. Martin Seligman?
Raymond Nathan just looks at him, incredulous.

Seligman did an experiment with dogs. He put 'em in a cage with a barrier running down the middle -one side of the cage was electrified, the other wasn't. At first when he shocked the dogs, they'd jump over the barrier to get away. And then he electrified both sides. There's no escape. No belief they could change anything. He'd open the door to the cage and they wouldn't even leave. That's learned helplessness -- that's what we're going to instill in this man. You'll see.
ANGLE ON A TERRIFIED ZUBAYDAH ON THE TV MONITOR PRE-LAP: sound of hammering.
EXT. DETENTION SITE GREEN - ANOTHER DAY - FLASHBACK
ANGLE ON A BOX OF BUGS, ALIVE AND SQUIRMING Mitchell builds a WOODEN BOX that resembles a coffin. He hammers in nails. Gary looks on. Ali approaches.
A coffin? You're gonna kill him?
Let's call it a confinement box. I got this one. I got a smaller one.
And you're gonna put him in there?
With these -- which based on my evaluation, he hates.
Mitchell shows Ali a box of bugs.
Are you out of your mind?
Ali turns to Gary.
We don't do this. You know this is against the law.
The most senior people have signed off on it.
We're putting him on trial. How are we gonna build a case against him?
Mitchell appears genuinely confused by this.
Who says he's getting a trial?
EXT. CENTRAL PARK - BETHESDA FOUNTAIN - PRESENT
Back to Ali and Dan.
I wanted to arrest them right there on the spot. I told the Bureau that-
What'd they say?
They briefed Director Mueller and told me to get the hell out of there. So, I did.
When was that?
Late May -- mid-May.

Because I'm finding records that say that -- well, Mitchell left a little while later -- early June -for R and R and some meetings at headquarters. But, we had one high value detainee in custody: Abu Zubaydah. And after Mitchell left, the CIA put him in isolation and didn't ask him a single question for 47 days.
Are you fucking kidding me? The whole country was on red alert that summer. If they were so concerned about the next attack -- why'd they stop asking questions?
Right, it doesn't make any sense.
Once the psychologists showed up, almost nothing did anymore.
EXT. WASHINGTON MONUMENT, D.C. - NIGHT Late evening in the nation's capital. INT. SENATOR FEINSTEIN'S OFFICE - NIGHT
